Inductive deployment estimates for clinical+IMU and IMU-only UPDRS-III prediction in Parkinson's disease: a clinical benchmark and two-site cross-cohort stress test on WearGait-PD

A pre-registered, lockbox-protected inductive benchmark; the first published cross-site number on WearGait-PD (two-site stress test); explicitly framed as a clinical+IMU hybrid for total UPDRS-III rather than a pure gait-IMU result.

Author block — to be completed

Affiliations / corresponding author / funding / COI — to be completed

Abstract

Background. The Movement Disorder Society Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale (MDS-UPDRS) Part III is the de-facto standard for clinical motor severity assessment in Parkinson's disease (PD), but in-clinic scoring is rater-dependent and infrequent. Inertial measurement unit (IMU) wearables promise objective continuous severity estimation, but published gait-IMU UPDRS-III regressors have been trained and evaluated on small single-site cohorts (typically N<30 LOOCV) and have not been benchmarked under strictly inductive subject-level evaluation on the largest available controlled-gait PD dataset.

Methods. We benchmark UPDRS-III regression on the WearGait-PD dataset (N = 178: 98 PD + 80 HC; 13 body-worn IMUs at 100 Hz; controlled gait, balance, and TUG tasks; two collection sites). We pre-register three canonical pipelines and report under strict inductive eval (subject-level LOOCV with a fold-local imputation/normalisation firewall, per-fold K=500 LightGBM-importance feature selection, and a 5-null-gate certification before any number is reported): (i) T1 — axial+truncal subscore (items 9–14, Schrag axial + body bradykinesia 3.14) via a per-item gated IMU composite (iter12); (ii) T3 — total UPDRS-III via a hybrid two-stage pipeline (Ridge on H&Y + three intake covariates → LightGBM on V2 IMU residual; iter5) — explicitly a clinical+IMU rather than IMU-only model; (iii) T3 transportability — leave-one-site-out (LOSO) two-way mean across the two collection sites (iter16; reported as a two-site stress test, not a generalization-bound estimator).

Results. The triple inductive estimate is T1 LOOCV CCC = 0.655 (95% CI [0.512, 0.753]; calibration slope = 0.48; MAE = 1.56; N = 94); T3 LOOCV CCC = 0.523 (calibration slope = 0.40; MAE = 7.53; N = 98). The IMU residual contribution at T3 is Δ = +0.114 CCC (paired bootstrap 95% CI [+0.042, +0.186]) over a clinical-features-only Stage 1 baseline of CCC = 0.409; an IMU-only LightGBM (no clinical Stage 1) achieves only CCC = 0.207, so the headline T3 figure should be read as a clinical+IMU hybrid in which the clinical staging variables are the dominant component. T3 LOSO two-way mean CCC = 0.341 (NLS→WPD = 0.419; WPD→NLS = 0.263) — the first published WearGait-PD cross-site number, but a single point estimate from N = 2 sites and not a transportability distribution. Both pipelines compress predicted dynamic range substantially (slopes 0.40–0.48), preserving rank-order concordance but underestimating departures from the cohort mean.

Conclusions. Internal LOOCV ceilings on this dataset are substantially below the apparent performance of pipelines that fail strict inductive eval (T1 transductive CCC = 0.859 collapses to 0.588; T3 0.757 → 0.267; same code path, identical N), and the LOOCV→LOSO gap of 0.18 CCC widens this further under cross-site shift. Cross-sectional T3 MAE = 7.53 is informative for population-level deployment but is not a within-subject change-detection number; comparison to the longitudinal MCID of 3.25 (Horváth 2015) is for scale, not for individual-change utility. Medication ON/OFF state is unannotated in the public WearGait-PD release and remains an unmodelled confounder. We position the work as a cautionary inductive benchmark and a first cross-site transportability stress test on WearGait-PD, not as a deployable gait-IMU UPDRS-III predictor.

Introduction

The MDS-UPDRS Part III is the operational standard for tracking motor severity in PD[1], but in-clinic scoring is sparse, expensive, and inter-rater variable, with a minimal clinically important difference of approximately 3.25 points[3]. Body-worn IMU sensors measuring linear acceleration and angular velocity during gait offer a path to dense, objective severity estimation that is patient-friendly and site-independent. The motivating clinical question is simple: given a few minutes of instrumented gait, can a deployable model predict UPDRS-III with clinically meaningful accuracy?

The published literature is small and methodologically heterogeneous. Hssayeni et al. (2021) report MAE = 5.95 with Pearson r = 0.79 on N = 24 subjects under LOOCV in a free-living context with different IMU placement [4]. Shuqair et al. (2024) report r = 0.89 (MAE ≈ 5.65) on N = 24 [5], again at LOOCV without slope or concordance reported. Both numbers are impressive on their face, but two structural concerns limit their interpretation as deployment ceilings. First, the sample size (N = 24) means a single LOOCV fold leaves N − 1 = 23 training subjects — repeated LOOCV at this scale is highly susceptible to optimistic bias from any pre-evaluation hyperparameter selection or feature pre-computation that touches the held-out subject. Second, neither study reports calibration slope or Lin's concordance coefficient (CCC[6]), so the question of whether predictions are proportional to truth (versus regressed toward the cohort mean) is not addressed.

WearGait-PD (Synapse syn61370558 / syn55105530)[11] is the largest open multi-IMU dataset with full per-subject MDS-UPDRS-III scores. It comprises N = 178 subjects (98 PD + 80 HC), 13 body-worn IMUs sampled at 100 Hz, and a battery of controlled gait and balance tasks (self-paced walk, hurried walk, Timed-Up-and-Go, balance, tandem gait). Subjects were collected at two sites that are identifiable from their subject IDs (with 70 subjects from site NLS and 28 from site WPD); we treat site as a nuisance factor for transportability analysis. To our knowledge no UPDRS-III regression benchmark has been published on this dataset under strict inductive evaluation.

Two clinical considerations frame our choice of endpoints. First, the axial+truncal subscore (T1 = items 9–14) is clinically central but particularly hard to capture in clinic: axial features are notoriously levodopa-resistant relative to appendicular bradykinesia and are the dominant drivers of falls and loss of independence in PD[2]. An objective home-monitorable axial estimator therefore addresses a need that medication-titration tools generally do not. Second, total UPDRS-III (T3) inherently caps what any gait-only IMU model can explain: 12 of the 18 Part III items measure non-gait phenomena (speech, facial expression, hand/finger bradykinesia, rest tremor, rigidity), so even an oracle gait classifier is bounded by the variance share that gait carries (Bound A in the Discussion). We treat T3 as a population/cohort-level endpoint and T1 as the more individually informative target at the per-subject level.

Throughout this work we make three contributions.

  1. An honest internal-validity ceiling for axial+truncal severity (T1 = items 9–14, the Schrag axial subscore[2] with body bradykinesia 3.14): LOOCV CCC = 0.6550 (95% CI [0.5122, 0.7526]; MAE = 1.561; N = 94) via a per-item gated composite (iter12). Each item is predicted by its own model variant, the per-item LOOCV out-of-fold predictions are summed, and the composite CCC is reported.
  2. An honest internal-validity ceiling for total UPDRS-III: LOOCV CCC = 0.523 (MAE = 7.53; N = 98) via a two-stage clinical+IMU hybrid (iter5). Stage 1 is a Ridge regression on H&Y stage and three intake covariates (years since diagnosis, sex, DBS status); Stage 2 is a LightGBM on the V2 IMU feature residual. The IMU residual contribution over a clinical-features-only Stage-1 baseline is Δ = +0.114 (paired bootstrap 95% CI [+0.042, +0.186], n_boot = 2 000); an IMU-only LightGBM (no clinical Stage 1) achieves only CCC = 0.207. The headline figure should therefore be read as a clinical+IMU result in which the clinical staging variables remain the dominant component; iter5 exceeds the IMU-only theoretical oracle ceiling (Bound A = 0.351; Discussion §2) precisely because Stage 1 carries non-IMU clinical signal.
  3. The first published cross-site number on WearGait-PD: T3 LOSO two-way mean CCC = 0.341 (NLS→WPD = 0.419; WPD→NLS = 0.263; single point estimate from N = 2 sites). The 0.18-CCC LOOCV→LOSO gap is the central result for clinical deployment: it quantifies the cost of moving from a single-site benchmark to a new collection site without retraining. We characterise this as a two-site stress test, not a generalization-bound estimator (a third site is the immediate priority).

We position the work as a cautionary inductive benchmark — a realistic deployment estimate under strict eval, not a SOTA claim — and back it with a pre-registered lockbox protocol, a 5-null-gate firewall against common leakage classes, and an extensive negative-results catalogue that triangulates why frozen healthy-population pretrained encoders, IMU feature-group expansions, and end-to-end deep architectures do not move the ceiling at this sample size.

Results

Cohort

The WearGait-PD dataset contains N = 178 subjects (98 PD, 80 HC) with PD age = 66.9 ± 8.3 years, sex = 64% male, years since diagnosis = 5.9 ± 6.1, and total UPDRS-III range [0, 59] (mean 24.4 ± 10.9). Of the 98 PD subjects, 94 had complete item 9–14 scores enabling T1 axial+truncal evaluation, and 98 had a recorded total UPDRS-III. Subjects were collected at two sites (with 70 subjects from site NLS and 28 from site WPD); the asymmetric site sizes are exploited for the LOSO transportability analysis.

Headline results — three canonical inductive pipelines

Table 2 summarises the three pre-registered headline numbers. Figure 1 shows the pipeline block diagrams; Figure 2 shows per-subject scatter plots for each.

T1 axial+truncal — per-item gated composite

The T1 composite is the sum of six per-item LOOCV out-of-fold prediction arrays, each from its own model variant pre-registered as a single iter8 lockbox batch (Table 3, Figure 3). Items split cleanly into two regimes: postural stability (item 12) is the strongest single item with LOOCV CCC = 0.5928; gait (item 10) and arising from chair (item 9) follow at 0.4755 and 0.4437; freezing of gait (item 11) and body bradykinesia (item 14) are intermediate; posture (item 13) is the weakest at 0.1169, consistent with item 13 being partly habitual/anatomical and capped by inter-rater scoring noise. The composite at CCC = 0.6550 reflects the summing of these per-item OOF predictions before evaluating concordance against sum-of-true.

Hypothesis-restricted submodels for tremor items (3.15 and 3.18, iter17)

The V2 feature pool (1 751 cols) draws statistical aggregates across all 13 sensors and full-recording windows. For items whose clinical signature is anatomically narrow (rest and postural tremor at the wrist, sustained over a short quiet-stance window), this broad pooling dilutes the relevant signal: a per-fold K = 500 LightGBM-importance selector cannot distinguish the rare wrist-tremor moments from amplitude variance in the 1 700+ irrelevant aggregates. We tested whether tight hypothesis-restricted feature sets (8–10 wrist-only spectral or burst-detection features per item, anchored on the clinically-relevant sensor + channel + time window) bypass this absorption when used either as the sole input (item_only) or with H&Y residualization (hy_residual_item_v2). Items {4, 6, 15, 16, 17, 18} were screened (5 seeds × 5-fold × 3 variants); the strict gate (Δ ≥ +0.05 vs published baseline AND seed_std < 0.02 across 5 seeds) was applied without post-hoc relaxation, and only items 15 and 18 passed both conditions. Item 17 (rest tremor amplitude, item_plus_v2 +0.217 ± 0.036) and item 16 (kinetic tremor, item_plus_v2 +0.179 ± 0.052) had positive Δ but failed the std condition; they are documented as supplementary borderline rather than lockboxed (see Limitations §9 for the std-gate rationale).

Both passers were lockboxed under a single coherent pre-registration (the same JSON file before any LOOCV ran) to avoid the multi-batch composite cherry-picking failure mode of iter11A. Item 15 (postural tremor) moved from a published baseline of CCC = -0.090 to LOOCV CCC = 0.1099 (MAE = 1.088; Δ = +0.200; seed std = 0.0065, N = 94). The 10 item-only features (4–7 Hz FreeAcc bandpower at L/R Wrist in Balance pre/post-instruction pauses, plus L/R asymmetry) are used WITHOUT V2; the K = 500 selector therefore sees only 10 candidates and cannot displace the relevant spectral channels. Item 18 (rest tremor constancy) moved from CCC = +0.250 to LOOCV CCC = 0.4858 (MAE = 0.887; Δ = +0.236; seed std = 0.0204, N = 93) under the hy_residual_item_v2 architecture: Stage-1 Ridge on H&Y stage carries the dominant severity backbone, Stage-2 LightGBM on (V2 ⊕ 8 wrist burst-detection features) operates on the H&Y residual where the small wrist set only competes for the un-explained variance. Item 18's +0.236 LOOCV CCC gain is the largest single-item improvement on this dataset since the iter6 gated composite. Both lifts are entirely consistent with the iter5 H&Y-residual mechanism reported above for T3, except that here the small clinical anchor is contributed by the item-specific feature set rather than by patient-level intake covariates.

The negative space is just as informative: at this N, broad augmentation of V2 (here: 255 features from the entirely-unused IMU channels Mag/VelInc/OriInc, iter17 Phase A1) failed both gates with sum-T1 Δ = −0.043 and a catastrophic −0.151 collapse on item 11 (FoG). The same K = 500 absorption mechanism that retired prior FoG-summary and HARNet attempts re-emerged when the small set was added on top of V2 inside an item_dedicated variant where the new dimensions then competed with the small dedicated FoG features. The pattern is therefore architectural rather than channel-specific: hypothesis-restricted feature sets succeed only when the architecture either bypasses V2 entirely or subordinates the small set to a residual signal carried by a strong Stage-1 backbone.

T3 total UPDRS — clinical residualization breaks the IMU-only ceiling

T3 prediction is a two-stage architecture (Pipeline 2; appendix Figure B). Stage 1 fits Ridge regression (α = 1.0) on H&Y stage (linear + 5 one-hot bins) and three patient intake covariates available before the gait session: years since diagnosis (cv_yrs; Pearson r with T3 = 0.32), sex (cv_sex), and DBS status (cv_dbs). Stage 2 fits a LightGBM regressor on the V2 IMU feature residual (target = T3 − T3̂_clinical) with per-fold K = 500 LGB-importance feature selection. The headline LOOCV CCC = 0.5227 (MAE = 7.525; N = 98) represents a paired-bootstrap-significant lift of Δ = +0.114 (95% CI [+0.042, +0.186], frac_positive = 1.000, n_boot = 2 000) over the iter3 hy_residual baseline (CCC = 0.4092), evaluated on the same N = 98 subjects.

The architecture breaks the theoretical IMU-only oracle ceiling Bound A = 0.351 (Figure 4) because the three intake covariates carry clinical staging information that is not present in the gait IMU stream. Importantly, all three covariates are recorded before the gait session as part of standard intake — there is no leakage from the assessment we are predicting. Because cv_dbs is included as a Stage-1 predictor and ~24% of the cohort carries DBS implants, we report a post-hoc DBS=0 vs DBS=1 stratification of the iter5 (and iter12) per-subject predictions in Table S4; T3 iter5 CCC is essentially identical across DBS strata (0.499 vs 0.496) but within-stratum calibration slopes (0.71, 0.75) are markedly steeper than the pooled slope (0.40), a Simpson-paradox-like effect driven by between-group mean shift.

Transportability — LOSO two-way mean

The WearGait-PD PD cohort is split unevenly across collection sites (with 70 subjects from site NLS and 28 from site WPD), which makes cross-site transport an asymmetric stress test. As an internal-validity sensitivity check, inverse-propensity weighting (IPW) applied to Stage 2 of the iter5 architecture under LOOCV reduces CCC from 0.5227 to 0.4694 (Δ = -0.053), confirming that cohort imbalance does not inflate the canonical headline. We report the IPW LOOCV result as a site-balanced lower bound (Table S1); iter5 (no IPW) remains the canonical LOOCV headline for internal validity.

The transportability headline is the iter16 LOSO two-way mean (Table 5, Figure 5). Trained on NLS (N = 70) and tested on WPD (N = 28), the iter5 architecture achieves CCC = 0.4192 (MAE = 6.42). The reverse direction (trained on WPD N = 28, tested on NLS N = 70) is harder, with CCC = 0.2627 (MAE = 9.98) — the smaller training set is partly responsible. The two-way mean CCC = 0.341 is the first published WearGait-PD transportability number. The 0.182-CCC gap between LOOCV and LOSO is the most important number for clinical deployment: it quantifies the cost of moving from a single-site benchmark to a new collection site without retraining.

Cross-dataset context

Hssayeni 2021 (MAE = 5.95, r = 0.79) and Shuqair 2024 (r = 0.89, MAE ≈ 5.65) report headline numbers that are stronger than ours on their own datasets — but both run on N = 24 subjects under LOOCV, neither reports CCC or calibration slope, and both differ from WearGait-PD in IMU placement and acquisition context. The Δ = 0.27 CCC gap that our own pipeline shows between transductive and strictly inductive evaluation at N = 94 (Table 7) is a useful prior on how much LOOCV at N = 24 can inflate apparent performance even when no protocol error is intended. We therefore position Table 6 as a contextual reference, not a head-to-head benchmark: our T3 LOOCV CCC = 0.5227 (MAE = 7.53) is the first inductively-honest number on a substantially larger controlled-gait dataset, and LOSO CCC = 0.341 is the first cross-site deployment estimate on any PD-IMU corpus we are aware of. Reproducing the prior numbers' headline accuracy under our protocol on those datasets is the right way to test whether the architectural difference or the protocol difference dominates the gap.

Why the inductive ceiling is so much lower than published apparent performance

The 0.859–0.588 = -0.271 CCC gap on T1 (and -0.490 on T3) shown in Figure 7 is not a quirk of feature engineering — it is the cost of two leakage classes that are easy to introduce by accident in small-N regression: (i) pre-computed target-derived structures (XGBRanker leaf indices, retrieval library memberships, prototype anchors) fitted on the full N before cross-validation, and (ii) hyperparameter tuning on the test-set vector (post-hoc temperature scaling, calibration grid search) reported on the same vector without nested cross-validation. We pre-empt these via a 5-null-gate firewall (Methods Box; appendix Figure E) and a single-shot pre-registered lockbox protocol (appendix Figure D).

Negative results — the N≈94 wall

Across iter6 (V2 + unsigned-asymmetry, V2 + event-axial), iter14 (FoG-summary scalars on items 9 and 12), and iter15 (frozen UKB OxWearables HARNet 2 048-d wrist embeddings[12]; ~700 K person-days SSL), no IMU feature-group expansion or frozen healthy-population pretrained encoder cleared the pre-lockbox gate (Δ ≥ +0.025 with seed std < 0.020 across 3–5 seeds; Table 8, Figure 8). The HARNet result is particularly informative: every one of 5 seeds was negative, with mean Δ = -0.0314. Together with prior null results from frozen MOMENT[13] and a healthy-only contrastive SSL encoder, this triangulates a structural conclusion: at N ≈ 94 PD subjects, frozen healthy-population pretrained encoders are orthogonal to within-PD severity at any embedding scale we have tested.

We read this catalogue as positive evidence that the LOOCV ceilings reported above are not artefacts of feature under-engineering. The wall is partly sample size — the within-PD cohort is small relative to the embedding rank of these encoders, so additional features compete for K = 500 LightGBM-importance slots and crowd out useful V2 moments — and partly biological, because gait simply does not carry the information needed to predict speech, facial expression, or arm-segment tremor regardless of N. Five end-to-end deep-learning architectures we evaluated earlier in development (1D-CNN, ResNet-1D, TCN, BiLSTM, and a Transformer encoder; trained directly on the 78-channel raw signal) all underfit at N ≈ 94 PD subjects and were dropped before lockboxing for the same reason: at this sample size the gradient-boosted trees on V2 features already dominate the achievable inductive ceiling. Both walls combine to flatten the achievable lift from feature-only innovation at this dataset scale, which is why the iter5 architectural lift comes from new signal (intake covariates) rather than from new features.

Multiplicity. Across the >20 feature-addition configurations explored across iter4–iter15 (catalogued informally in our project notes), the pre-lockbox gate of Δ ≥ +0.025 with seed std < 0.020 across 3–5 seeds is conservative relative to per-seed sampling noise (per-seed CCC SD ≈ 0.06 at this N), giving an estimated per-comparison Type-I rate well under 5 % under H₀ even without an explicit Benjamini–Hochberg correction. The negative-direction sign of HARNet, MOMENT, and HC-SSL (mean Δ = -0.031, -0.000, -0.012 respectively) further rules out that any of these would have survived a formal FDR-controlled screen. We list these here for transparency rather than as multiplicity-corrected p-values; none of the per-iteration null results were used to select the canonical pipelines.

Discussion

What the triple inductive ceiling means for deployment

The single number a deployment-minded reader should take from this paper is the 0.18-CCC drop from internal LOOCV (0.523) to two-site LOSO (0.341) on T3. Even an inductively-honest internal benchmark substantially overstates what a model trained at one site achieves at a new site, and we observe this gap in a tightly controlled multi-IMU protocol — free-living deployment will widen it further, not narrow it. With only two collection sites this is a single point estimate, not a sample from a transportability distribution; we characterise it as a two-site stress test rather than a generalization-bound estimator. Clinical deployment must plan for site-level fine-tuning, federated training, or explicit cohort-shift correction; the deployed CCC is the LOSO figure, not the LOOCV figure.

Both headline pipelines exhibit calibration slopes of 0.48 (T1) and 0.40 (T3), meaning predictions are systematically compressed toward the cohort mean. A subject whose true severity is 1 SD above the cohort is predicted at roughly half an SD above; the models preserve rank ordering and concordance but underestimate the amplitude of departures from the mean. This is the failure mode CCC was chosen to penalise (Pearson r alone would mask it), and it is why we report calibration slope alongside CCC in every headline table. Operationally, the practical consequence is that severe (and very mild) phenotypes will be regressed toward the cohort centre — a model deployed for trial-enrichment of severe cases would under-select. Stratifying by DBS status reveals a Simpson-paradox-like pattern: within-stratum slopes are 0.71 (DBS=0) and 0.75 (DBS=1) versus the pooled 0.40 (Table S4); the pooled slope reflects between-group severity-mean shift more than within-group dynamic-range compression, and the apparent compression is partly an artefact of pooling clinically distinct subgroups.

The other two anchors then position what each headline is good for. T1 axial+truncal CCC = 0.655 (MAE = 1.56) on a target whose theoretical range is 0–24 (six items scored 0–4) and whose observed cohort mean is 4.1 ± 2.8 (SD) is a usable subscore-level rank estimator: the typical absolute error is small relative to clinical scoring noise on a clinically central, fall-relevant subscore. T3 total UPDRS CCC = 0.523 (MAE = 7.53) is best read as a hybrid clinical+IMU estimate (Stage 1 carries most of the signal; the IMU residual contributes Δ = +0.114 CCC over a clinical-features-only baseline) rather than as a gait-IMU result; absent intake covariates, the deployment ceiling collapses to the iter3 baseline of CCC = 0.409, or to CCC = 0.207 for an IMU-only LightGBM (B1 baseline; Table 4).

Cross-sectional MAE is informative for population-level deployment but is not directly comparable to the minimal-clinically-important-difference (MCID) of 3.25[3], which is a within-subject longitudinal change anchor (Horváth 2015 derived it from individual change scores in PD patients followed over time). T3 MAE = 7.53 is roughly 2.3× the MCID in absolute scale-points, but whether the model can detect a one-MCID change in the same patient over time requires longitudinal follow-up data we do not have. We therefore use the MCID only as a reference scale for the cross-sectional MAE, not as a per-patient change-detection benchmark. The realistic deployment use cases for these pipelines are population-level: cohort enrichment for clinical trials (selecting subjects whose IMU-implied severity matches an inclusion stratum), automated monitoring for trend detection or adverse-event flagging in long-term remote follow-up, and pre-screening for in-clinic full-UPDRS examination. The T1 axial+truncal head (CCC = 0.655, MAE = 1.56) is the closer match to per-subject use, given its lower error in absolute scale-points and its alignment with fall-risk-relevant symptomatology — though calibration-slope compression still applies.

Why theoretical ceilings matter

The IMU-only oracle ceiling Bound A = 0.351 (perfect T1 prediction + mean residual) is derived purely from the variance decomposition of T3 into items 9–14 (var(T1)/var(T3) ≈ 7.7%) and the items not directly observable from gait (12 of 18 UPDRS-III items, including speech, facial expression, rest tremor, and rigidity). Iter5 exceeds Bound A by injecting non-IMU clinical signal at Stage 1, not by improving the IMU model. The mechanism is straightforward in a small-N, high-p regression: Stage 1 (Ridge on H&Y + intake covariates) absorbs coarse clinical severity from cheap, low-variance, prior-known patient state; Stage 2 (LightGBM on the V2 IMU residual) then has only the within-stratum variance to model, which is what the IMU is actually informative about. This is the central architectural insight: at this sample size and feature regime, gain comes from new signal (intake covariates, H&Y stage) rather than from new features (IMU-derived). The negative-results catalogue (Table 8, Figure 8) is consistent with that interpretation.

Comparison with prior gait-IMU UPDRS-III work

Hssayeni 2021[4] and Shuqair 2024[5] achieve lower MAE (5.95 and ~5.65) and higher r (0.79 and 0.89) than our T3 numbers, but each operates on N = 24 subjects under LOOCV. Two cautions are warranted before treating those numbers as deployment ceilings. First, neither paper reports CCC or calibration slope, so a model that systematically regresses toward the cohort mean would still register an apparently high r. Second, at N = 24, even modest test-set re-use across hyperparameter sweeps can inflate LOOCV apparent performance substantially — the 0.27 CCC gap on our own pipeline at N = 94 (Table 7) puts a useful prior on this concern. Our larger N and stricter inductive protocol probably explain the gap with their reported numbers as much as any architectural difference.

Limitations — what this benchmark does NOT support

We state the limitations as explicit non-claims so they are not mistaken for soft hedging. (1) No per-patient titration. T3 MAE = 7.53 (cross-sectional) is roughly 2.3× the longitudinal MCID of 3.25 in absolute scale-points; the error budget is too large for individual-patient medication adjustments. The T1 axial+truncal ceiling (MAE = 1.56) is closer to per-patient utility for axial symptomatology specifically, but is not a substitute for clinical exam. (2) No prospective validation. All numbers in this paper are retrospective on a single archived dataset; the LOSO CCC = 0.341 is a two-site stress test, not a field-validated deployment number. (3) Cross-sectional MAE is not a change-detection metric. The MCID of 3.25[3] is a within-subject longitudinal change anchor; we use it only as a reference scale for cross-sectional MAE and we cannot infer per-patient change-detection accuracy from cross-sectional error. (4) Single dataset for development. All three pipelines were screened, lockboxed, and reported on WearGait-PD; we have not demonstrated transfer to Hssayeni, Shuqair, or any other PD-IMU corpus, and the iter5 architecture's lift may not generalize to datasets without H&Y and intake covariates of comparable quality. (5) Two-site LOSO is a stress test, not a transportability distribution. With only two sites, the LOSO two-way mean is a single point estimate; a third site would let us distinguish site-specific noise from a true cohort-shift law. The asymmetry between the two directions (NLS→WPD = 0.419 vs WPD→NLS = 0.263) is partly attributable to imbalanced training-set sizes (70 vs 28). (6) Controlled gait, not free-living. WearGait-PD uses scripted tasks; subjects know they are being assessed and tend to recruit attentional/visuomotor compensation that transiently masks mild bradykinesia (paradoxical kinesia / Hawthorne effect). Free-living deployment is therefore expected to show lower baseline concordance than even our LOSO numbers suggest, not higher; LOSO does not measure this offset. (7) Clinical intake covariates carry most of the iter5 signal. The iter5 architecture is a clinical+IMU hybrid in which Stage 1 (Ridge on H&Y + intake) carries the bulk of predictive variance (CCC = 0.409) and Stage 2 (IMU residual) contributes Δ = +0.114; if H&Y, years-since-diagnosis, sex, and DBS status are unavailable at deployment (e.g., remote monitoring without recent clinical visits), the relevant ceilings are iter3 (CCC = 0.409) or, in the fully-IMU-only case, the B1 LightGBM baseline (CCC = 0.207; Table 4). (8) Calibration slope is below 0.5 on both targets. Predictions are systematically compressed toward the cohort mean; severe and very mild phenotypes are individualised poorly. This is the failure mode CCC was chosen to penalise — we report it openly, and any deployment that depends on absolute predicted scores (rather than ranks) should account for it. (9) Medication ON/OFF state is unannotated in WearGait-PD. MDS-UPDRS-III scores are heavily medication-state-dependent (levodopa pharmacodynamics drive substantial within-patient variance); the residual variance unexplained by our model is therefore confounded by unobserved medication state. We cannot rule out that a fraction of the LOOCV→LOSO gap reflects between-site differences in scoring conventions for medication state rather than pure cohort shift, and we cannot stratify the headline numbers by ON/OFF condition. (10) DBS subgroup not separately reported. DBS implant presence is included as a Stage-1 covariate (cv_dbs); we report a post-hoc DBS=0 vs DBS=1 stratification of the iter5 and iter12 lockbox per-subject predictions in Table S4. The substantive finding is that T3 iter5 CCC is nearly identical across strata (0.499 vs 0.496) — the model's rank concordance is invariant to DBS status — but within-stratum calibration slopes (0.71, 0.75) are markedly steeper than the joint slope (0.40), indicating the pooled slope reflects between-group mean shift rather than within-group dynamic-range compression. T1 iter12 shows a modest CCC gap across strata (0.643 vs 0.555). DBS ON/OFF state during the gait recording is not annotated separately from DBS device presence in the public release, so we cannot stratify further.

Future work

Three concrete directions are ranked by the constraint they would relax. First, a third collection site — independent of NLS and WPD — would convert the LOSO two-way mean from a single point into a distribution and let us bound site-noise variance separately from a true cohort-shift law. Second, cross-dataset transfer of the iter5 architecture to the MJFF Levodopa Response Study (which carries the same UPDRS-III + IMU pairing) is the immediate cross-corpus test; success there would establish that clinical residualization with intake covariates is a transferable architectural pattern rather than a WearGait-PD artefact. Third, the 80 healthy controls in WearGait-PD remain unused in the canonical pipelines: leveraging them as hold-out distributional anchors for prediction-reliability calibration (rather than as ranking labels, which leaks) would add per-prediction confidence intervals without contaminating the regression signal.

Methods

Dataset

WearGait-PD (Synapse syn61370558 / syn55105530)[11] contains 178 subjects (98 PD, 80 HC) wearing 13 body-worn Movella DOT IMUs (head, sternum, lumbar, bilateral wrists, bilateral upper arms, bilateral thighs, bilateral shanks, bilateral feet) sampled at 100 Hz, performing self-paced walk, hurried walk, Timed-Up-and-Go (TUG), balance, and tandem gait tasks (with mat and turn variants). Subjects were collected at two sites identifiable from their SID prefix (with 70 subjects from site NLS and 28 from site WPD). The clean PD-only subject-level split (seed = 20260309) is at results/paper3_split.json. T1 evaluation uses the 94 PD subjects with complete item 9–14 scores; T3 uses the 98 PD subjects with a recorded total UPDRS-III.

Preprocessing and feature extraction

Per-recording z-score normalisation is applied to the 13 sensors × 6 channels (Acc XYZ + Gyr XYZ) = 78-channel signal. The window pipeline uses 1 000-sample (10 s) windows with 500-sample (50 %) stride. Subject-level features used in the canonical pipelines are loaded from results/ablation_v3_features.csv (~1 751 V2 features per subject, including time-domain moments, frequency-domain summaries, and per-task task-segmented statistics). The TUG phase-segmented features used in some per-item lockboxes are 6-phase (sit-to-stand, walk-out, turn, walk-back, turn, stand-to-sit) segmentations around the lumbar acceleration-magnitude spike, yielding 421 additional features.

Pipeline 1 — T1 iter12 honest (per-item gated composite)

Implementation: compose_t1_iter12_honest.py. The composer loads six pre-registered LOOCV out-of-fold prediction arrays for items 9–14 from a single iter8 lockbox batch (timestamp 20260430_143044, files results/lockbox_peritem_{9..14}_*_20260430_143044.json), sums them into a composite OOF prediction per subject, then evaluates concordance against the sum-of-true. The per-item variants are: item 9 = hy_residual_item; item 11 = item_dedicated; items 10, 12, 13, 14 = item_plus_v2 (Table 3). Single-iter8-batch sourcing prevents the composite-level cherry-picking inflation we have characterised in earlier per-item-best composites.

Each per-item LightGBM model uses identical hyperparameters: n_estimators = 500, learning_rate = 0.05, num_leaves = 15, min_data_in_leaf = 10, feature_fraction = 0.8, bagging_fraction = 0.8 (freq = 5), reg_alpha = 0.1, reg_lambda = 0.3, device = "cpu" (LightGBM GPU is 2.2× slower at N < 200 on this dataset). Three random seeds are averaged.

Pipeline 2 — T3 iter5 clinical (two-stage residualization)

Implementation: run_t3_iter5_clinical.py --mode lockbox --feature_set A3_tier1. Stage 1 is a Ridge regression (α = 1.0, fit_intercept = True) on 9 features = 6 H&Y representations (linear NaN→0, plus 5 one-hot bins) + 3 intake covariates: cv_yrs (years since diagnosis, from intake chart review), cv_sex, and cv_dbs (binary indicator of an implanted deep-brain stimulator at enrolment; this encodes device presence, not the on/off state during the gait task — all WearGait-PD sessions were recorded with the stimulator left in its standard clinical state, but ON/OFF state is not separately annotated in the public release). H&Y stage in the WearGait-PD release was assigned by the enrolling movement-disorders clinician at intake from chart review and bedside neurological exam, not derived from the UPDRS-III item scores we are predicting; we therefore treat it as an intake covariate rather than a target leak, consistent with WearGait-PD documentation. All Stage 1 features come from results/ablation_v3_features.csv. Stage 2 is a LightGBM regressor[7] (same hyperparameters as Pipeline 1) on the V2 IMU feature residual (target = T3 − T3̂_clinical) with per-fold K = 500 LGB-importance feature selection inside the train fold (appendix Figure C). The K = 500 cap was inherited unchanged from the iter4 / iter5 lockboxed configurations (5-fold-screened against K ∈ {200, 500, 1000, all}, with K = 500 the 5-fold optimum at the time of iter5 lockboxing); the LOOCV was then run exactly once on this frozen K and not re-tuned. LOOCV with N = 98, three random seeds (42, 1337, 7), per-subject prediction = mean across seeds. Paired bootstrap of (iter5 − iter3 baseline) over n_boot = 2 000 subject-level resamples (seed = 42) yields the lift Δ = +0.114 (95% CI [+0.042, +0.186]; frac > 0 = 1.000).

Pipeline 3 — T3 iter16 LOSO + LOOCV-IPW

Implementation: run_t3_iter16_site_ipw.py --mode lockbox. Stage 1 is bit-identical to Pipeline 2. Stage 2 LightGBM adds a per-fold sample weight = N_train / (2 · N_site_train) (deterministic count-based inverse-propensity weighting[9],[10]; no propensity-model logistic regression; no clipping). Two evaluation branches: (a) LOSO transportability — single split per direction × 3 seeds. NLS→WPD: train on N = 70 NLS subjects, test on N = 28 WPD; WPD→NLS: train on N = 28, test on N = 70. Two-way mean = (0.4192 + 0.2627) / 2 = 0.341. The IPW formula collapses to uniform weighting within single-site training, so the LOSO branch reports the iter5 architecture under cohort shift. (b) LOOCV-IPW — LOOCV with per-fold IPW on Stage 2; sensitivity Δ vs iter5 = -0.053 (Table S1).

Theoretical IMU-only ceiling (Bound A)

The IMU-only oracle ceiling Bound A is derived from a variance decomposition of T3 into the gait-observable subscore T1 (items 9–14, the Schrag axial+truncal items observable from instrumented gait) and the residual R = T3 − T1 (items 1–8 and 15–18, including speech, facial expression, hand/finger bradykinesia, rest tremor, and rigidity, which are not directly observable from gait IMU). On the N = 89 PD subjects with all 18 items recorded, var(T1)/var(T3) ≈ 7.7 %, so even an oracle gait classifier with perfect T1 prediction (T1̂ = T1) and a constant R̂ = mean(R) achieves a maximum CCC of 0.351 against true T3. The full set of bounds (iter1 derivation): Bound D (perfect-T1 → T3 max, the upper theoretical limit) = 0.683; Bound A (oracle T1 + mean R, IMU-only realistic max) = 0.351; Bound E (inductive shrinkage T1_pred → T3, what an inductive T1 model can actually deliver under shrinkage) = 0.171. Iter5 reaches CCC = 0.523, exceeding Bound A by injecting non-IMU clinical signal (H&Y stage and three intake covariates) at Stage 1 — this is the architectural insight the paper turns on.

Inductive firewall (inductive_lib.py)

The fold-local helpers enforce that anything that "fits" on training data is fit inside the fold: FoldImputer fits the median per feature on the train fold (NaN → 0 for all-NaN columns); FoldNormalizer z-scores using train-fold mean and SD (with ε = 1e-8 floor for zero-variance columns); FoldSeverityBins derives quartile cutpoints from y_train only. No global imputers, cohort-wide z-scores, pre-computed ranks, anchors, or prototypes are used anywhere in the canonical pipelines.

Methods Box: 5-null gate

Before any new pipeline is permitted to lockbox, it must pass five sanity nulls (appendix Figure E):

  1. Scrambled-label sanity. Shuffle train PD targets; expect test CCC ≈ 0. Catches most target-leak bugs.
  2. SID-shuffle before cache join. Shuffle subject IDs on the feature side; expect CCC ≈ 0. Catches leaks via cache join keys.
  3. Canary-feature injection. Inject a feature with value 999.0 into the test fold only; assert the model cannot use it. Catches accidental train/test smoothing.
  4. Library-exclusion assertion. For any kNN/retrieval architecture, assert that the test SID is not in the retrieval pool. Catches transductive leakage in nearest-neighbour or anchor-based methods.
  5. Transductive sanity (positive control). Intentionally leak the target into a feature and confirm CCC ≈ 0.85; this verifies the architecture is capable of learning when given the answer.

Concrete leakage citations from our own audit: run_compression_ablation.py:1015 fitted XGBRanker[8] on all 178 subjects globally; the leaf indices thereby encoded test-fold rank, costing ΔCCC = 0.343 on T1 5-fold (transductive 0.868 → inductive 0.525 in audit reproduction). run_calibration_v2.py:861 tuned a temperature grid on the same N = 94 LOOCV prediction vector it then evaluated; calibration slope was pinned to 1.000 by construction. Both classes of leakage are now blocked by the firewall; their detection is what motivates Pipeline 1's "single iter8 batch" sourcing rule.

Certification of canonical pipelines. The pipelines reported in Table 2 (iter12, iter5, iter16) were each rebuilt on top of inductive_lib.py with all five nulls executed before any LOOCV/LOSO headline was reported. The Table 7 leakage demonstration uses the older P5 SSL ranking pipeline (run_compression_ablation.py) reproduced under both protocols specifically so that the inductive→transductive gap is observable on a clean code-path comparison; the canonical iter12/iter5/iter16 pipelines have no Table-7-style transductive variant because the leaky operations have been removed at the source.

Pre-registered lockbox protocol

Pre-registration commitment. For each of the three canonical pipelines reported in this paper, the configuration (model class, hyperparameters, feature set, seeds, and CV protocol) was frozen and serialized into a pre-registration JSON (with script SHA + git SHA) before the LOOCV (or LOSO) run was launched. The LOOCV/LOSO run was then executed exactly once on that frozen configuration and the result reported regardless of outcome. We did not iterate on the pre-registered configuration after seeing its LOOCV/LOSO output, and we did not select the published pipeline by comparing LOOCV/LOSO scores across multiple lockbox candidates. The full workflow is: 5-fold CV is used to screen configurations across many candidates; the winning 5-fold configuration is pre-registered into a JSON; then LOOCV (or LOSO) is run once on that pre-registered configuration (appendix Figure D). This is not a registered report — the lockboxes were created and executed in our own repository before journal submission rather than time-stamped with a third-party registry — but the pre-registration JSONs are immutable, signed-by-SHA artifacts that can be inspected and re-run for byte-equivalent reproduction. Files: results/preregistration_t1_iter12_honest_*.json, results/preregistration_t3_iter5_20260502_171604.json, results/preregistration_t3_iter16_site_ipw_*.json.

Statistical analysis

All headline metrics are Lin's concordance correlation coefficient[6], calibration slope (linear regression of true on predicted), MAE, and Pearson r. We pre-register CCC rather than r as the primary metric because CCC penalises mean shifts and amplitude compression that Pearson r ignores: a model that systematically regresses toward the cohort mean (a known failure mode in small-N severity regression) can register a deceptively high r while its CCC collapses, so r alone would not distinguish a useful predictor from a calibrated one. CCC confidence intervals are subject-level percentile bootstrap, n_boot = 2000. Comparator deltas (iter5 − iter3, iter16 LOOCV-IPW − iter5) use paired bootstrap over the same subject indices.

Code and data availability

WearGait-PD is available via Synapse (syn61370558 / syn55105530) under the dataset's data use agreement. All analysis code and pre-registration JSONs are available in the project repository; reproducer commands are listed in Table P1. The master/slave split places thin Python on the master and the full ML stack (PyTorch, LightGBM, XGBoost, MOMENT, sktime) on a CUDA-equipped slave; the gpu.sh wrapper synchronises code, runs the script remotely, and pulls results.

References

  1. Goetz CG et al. Movement Disorder Society-sponsored revision of the Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale (MDS-UPDRS): Scale presentation and clinimetric testing results. Movement Disorders 23, 2129–2170 (2008).
  2. Schrag A, Sampaio C, Counsell N & Poewe W. Minimal clinically important change on the unified Parkinson's disease rating scale. Movement Disorders 21, 1200–1207 (2006).
  3. Horváth K et al. Minimal clinically important difference on the Motor Examination part of MDS-UPDRS. Parkinsonism & Related Disorders 21, 1421–1426 (2015).
  4. Hssayeni MD, Jimenez-Shahed J, Burack MA & Ghoraani B. Symptom-based, dual-channel LSTM network for the estimation of unified Parkinson's disease rating scale III. Biomedical Signal Processing and Control 70, 103009 (2021).
  5. Shuqair M et al. (2024). Wearable-derived gait features for Parkinson's disease motor severity estimation. (See cross-dataset table for protocol caveats.)
  6. Lin LI. A concordance correlation coefficient to evaluate reproducibility. Biometrics 45, 255–268 (1989).
  7. Ke G et al. LightGBM: A highly efficient gradient boosting decision tree. NeurIPS 30, 3146–3154 (2017).
  8. Chen T & Guestrin C. XGBoost: A scalable tree boosting system. KDD 785–794 (2016).
  9. van der Laan MJ & Robins JM. Unified Methods for Censored Longitudinal Data and Causality. Springer (2003). [IPW formalism]
  10. Stürmer T et al. A review of the application of propensity score methods yielded increasing use, advantages in specific settings, but not substantially different estimates compared with conventional multivariable methods. J Clin Epidemiol 59, 437–447 (2006).
  11. Adams JL et al. WearGait-PD: A multi-site IMU dataset for Parkinson's disease gait analysis (Synapse syn61370558 / syn55105530). Sage Bionetworks (data set, 2024).
  12. Yuan H, Chan S, Creagh AP, Tong C, Acquah A, Clifton DA, Doherty A. Self-supervised learning for human activity recognition using 700,000 person-days of wearable data. npj Digital Medicine 7, 91 (2024). [HARNet/UKB OxWearables; we use the frozen 2048-d wrist embedding here.]
  13. Goswami M, Szafraniec L, Choudhry K, Cai L, Li Y, Dubrawski A. MOMENT: A family of open time-series foundation models. ICML (2024).

Appendix — illustrative pipeline schematics

The figures below (Figures A–F) are illustrative schematics intended to make the pipeline mechanics accessible to clinical readers. Numeric labels embedded in the schematics (CCC, MAE, N) are loaded directly from the lockbox JSONs; the box layouts and arrows are illustrative.
